coredata.dc:title: Modification of g-C3N4 nanosheets by carbon quantum dots for highly efficient photocatalytic generation of hydrogen
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Kui
Su, Feng-Yun
Zhang, Wei-De
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited onto graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheets (CNNS) to form CNNS/CQDs composites. The prepared CNNS/CQDs composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation than pure CNNS do. The CNNS/CQDs-7 sample displays the highest photocatalytic performance, with H2 production rate of 116.1μmolh−1, which is three times of that over pure CNNS (37.8μmolh−1). Photoluminescence spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis reveal that the CQDs are favorable for trapping electrons and promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CNNS. A possible photocatalytic mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic performance for H2 generation over CNNS/CQDs will be proposed in this paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic carbon nitride
Carbon quantum dots
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen generation

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs; (B) SEM image of CNNS; (C) TEM image of CNNS after photocatalytic reaction; TEM (D) and HRTEM (E-G) images of CNNS/CQDs-7.
(A) XRD patterns of CNNS and CNNS/CQDs composites with different contents of CQDs; (B) FTIR spectra of CNNS, CQDs and CNNS/CQDs-7.
XPS spectra of CNNS/CQDs-7, (A) survey spectrum; high resolution XPS spectra of (B) O 1s and (C) N 1s of CNNS/CQDs-7; (D) high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s of pure CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs; (B) PL spectra of CQDs excited by light with different wavelengths.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CNNS and CNNS/CQDs composites with different contents of CQDs; (B) (αhν)2 versus hν curves of CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7; (C) PL spectra of the CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 under exciation with wavelength of 315nm.

(A) The amount of H2 generation and (B) H2 production rates over CNNS and CNNS/CQDs composites with different contents of CQDs in trithanolamine solution; (C) Cycle runs of the photocatalytic H2 production over CNNS/CQDs-7.

(A) Current-potential curves of the CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 electrodes in 0.50M Na2SO4 solution under illumination; (B) on-off cycles of the CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 electrodes under Xe lamp illumination in 0.50M Na2SO4 solution.
The mechanism of visible light driven photocatalytic generation of H2 over CNNS/CQDs composite catalyst.
Comparison of photocatalytic generation of hydrogen over the catalysts based on g-C3N4 and CQDs.

row.entry: Thermal condensation of urea and CDots

head.ce:title: Modification of g-C3N4 nanosheets by carbon quantum dots for highly efficient photocatalytic generation of hydrogen

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, South China University of Technology, 381 Wushan Road, Guangzhou 510640, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots-modified graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets (CNNS/CQDs) catalysts with high and stable activity for photocatalytic generation of hydrogen through water splitting were successfully constructed.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were deposited onto graphite-like carbon nitride nanosheets (CNNS) to form CNNS/CQDs composites. The prepared CNNS/CQDs composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation than pure CNNS do. The CNNS/CQDs-7 sample displays the highest photocatalytic performance, with H2 production rate of 116.1μmolh−1, which is three times of that over pure CNNS (37.8μmolh−1). Photoluminescence spectroscopy and photoelectrochemical (PEC) analysis reveal that the CQDs are favorable for trapping electrons and promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in CNNS. A possible photocatalytic mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic performance for H2 generation over CNNS/CQDs will be proposed in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar photocatalytic generation of H2 through water splitting over semiconductor photocatalysts has become a promising technology to cope with global energy crisis and environmental pollution [1–3]. Since Wang firstly reported the discovery of graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) for visible light photocatalytic water splitting in 2009 [4], this metal-free semiconductor photocatalyst with excellent thermal and chemical stability, accompanied with proper conjugated band structure has been identified as a robust scaffold for photocatalysis [5–7]. However, small specific surface area and high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs of g-C3N4 result in low photocatalytic efficiency [8,9]. In order to solve these problems, many strategies have been developed, including enlarging surface area of g-C3N4 by etching [10], or making g-C3N4 with different morphologies such as porous microspheres [11], tubes [12], nanosheets [13–15] etc. and constructing heterojunctions with other semiconductors, like Ag3VO4/g-C3N4 [16], Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [17], In2O3/g-C3N4 [18], S/g-C3N4 [19] and CdS/g-C3N4 [20] to improve the quantum efficiency. In recent years, metal-free carbonaceous materials with excellent conductivity such as graphene oxide [21,22], graphene [23–25], carbon nanotubes [26,27] and mesoporous carbon have been used as electron acceptors by coupling with g-C3N4 to separate the electron-hole pairs [28]. Nevertheless, it remains a great challenge to improve visible light photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4-based photocatalysts for commercial application.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with size smaller than 10nm, are a new kind of quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanocarbons comprising amorphous to nanocrystalline cores with typical graphite carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions. In recent years, CQDs have been successfully applied to chemical sensing [29], biosensing [30], bioimaging [31] and electrocatalysis [32] due to their unique properties, such as ultra-small size, low toxicity, water solubility, high photochemical stability and good electrical conductivity. Moreover, being capable of harvesting visible light and converting it to shorter wavelength light through up-conversion, and having unique photo-induced electron transfer property and different emissive traps on the surface of CQDs because of the quantum effect, CQDs have become a perfect material used in photocatalysis. So far, many photocatalysts with CQDs as an active component, like CdS/CQDs [33], TiO2/CQDs [34,35], and Bi2WO6/CQDs [36] have been employed for degradation of RhB under solar-simulated light irradiation. The CQDs in these composites play an important role in enhancing photocatalytic properties, such as utilizing the full spectrum of sunlight upon the up-conversion effect, exhibiting excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties due to the conjugated π structure and facilitating the absorption of organic dyes. Nevertheless, reports on CQDs-doped semiconductors for highly efficient photocatalytic hydrogen evolution remain rare.

Given the excellent properties of CQDs, they are promising candidates to enhance the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4. Here, we report the synthesis of the CNNS/CQDs composites and their highly improved photocatalytic properties for hydrogen generation through water splitting. Compared with traditional bulk structure, g-C3N4 nanosheets possess higher photocatalytic performance due to their larger specific surface area as well as more active sites. Therefore, in this work, we prepared two-dimensional g-C3N4 nanosheets using a dicyandiamide-blowing method with NH4Cl as a gas template [13]. Then, the CNNS/CQDs hybrid materials were prepared via a facile solvothermal reaction in ethanol. The morphologies, structures and optical properties of the prepared photocatalysts were investigated in details. The photocatalytic properties of the CNNS/CQDs composites were evaluated by photocatalytic production of H2 from triethanolamine aqueous solution under UV–vis light (λ>420nm). It is found that the g-C3N4 nanosheets modified by CQDs show higher photocatalytic efficiency than the pure g-C3N4. Moreover, the optimized CNNS/CQDs composite exhibited H2 evolution rate as high as 116.1μmolh−1, which is much more superior to that of the previous reports [13,37]. The enhancement mechanism of the composite photocatalysts will be studied as well.

Dicyandiamide (DC, 98%) was purchased from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium chloride (NH4Cl, 99.5%) was obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory (Guangzhou, China). Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O, Pt≥37.5%) and 6-aminohexanoic acid (AHA, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA, 99.5%), ethanol (99.7%) and isopropanol (99.7%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%) was purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Triethanolamine (TEOA, 85%) was purchased from Tianjing Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. (Tianjing, China). The deionized (DI) water was produced from Pure Water System (GWA-UN, Beijing, China).

The g-C3N4 nanosheets were synthesized by a thermal polymerization strategy according to a previous report [13]. In a typical procedure, 2g DC and 10g NH4Cl were dissolved in 100mL DI water under stirring until a transparent solution was formed, then it was dried at 100°C for 12h. The final white powder was annealed at 550°C for 4h. After cooling down to room temperature, g-C3N4 nanosheets were obtained.

CQDs were synthesized by the “bottom-up” method via thermal treatment of molecular precursors [38]. Firstly, 2g AHA was dissolved in 5mL DI water. 0.5g of NaOH was then added, followed by stirring for 1h. After that, the mixed solution was dried under vacuum at 80°C for 12h. The obtained 6-aminohexanoic sodium solid was washed by absolute ethanol to eliminate the residual sodium hydroxide. Secondly, the above 6-aminohexanoic sodium and 1g CA were mixed in 5mL DI water under stirring for 2h. The CA-AHA Na was precipitated after adding the above mixed aqueous solution into 200mL isopropanol, and then dried under vacuum at 40°C for 2h. Thirdly, the dried CA-AHA Na salt was calcined at 300°C for 2h in a muffle furnace. The obtained dark brown solid was dissolved in 5mL DI water. The CQDs were precipitated by adding extra acetone into the solution. The purified CQDs were acidized by 2mL of 36.5% concentrated hydrochloric acid. Finally, the precipitated brown CQDs were dried under vacuum at 40°C for 2h. The obtained CQDs were dispersed in ethanol with concentration of 0.8mgmL−1.

CNNS/CQDs were prepared by a simple process in which 90mg g-C3N4 nanosheets were added into certain volume of absolute ethanol under ultrasonic treatment for 1h. Then, the composites were prepared by changing the volume of CQDs ethanol solution (2, 4, 7 and 10mL) added into the above solution with the final volume of 40mL. The obtained samples were labeled as CNNS/CQDs-2, CNNS/CQDs-4, CNNS/CQDs-7 and CNNS/CQDs-10, respectively. After vigorous stirring for 1h, the above suspension was transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for further solvothermal reaction at 90°C for 2h. The as-synthesized light gray products were collected after cooling down to room temperature and vacuum-dried for 2h.

The phase composition and crystal structures of the samples were characterized by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker GADDS diffractometer). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F) with an accelerating voltage of 300kV was used to observe the morphologies of the samples. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were taken using an IR Affiniy-1 FTIR spectrometer. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were recorded with a UV–vis spectrometer (Hitachi U-3010) with an integrating sphere scanning from 200 to 800nm using BaSO4 as a reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The chemical status and surface chemical composition of the prepared samples were measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, USA).

Photocatalytic water splitting reaction of the photocatalysts was conducted in a Pyrex top-irradiation reaction vessel which connected to a closed glass gas circulation system (Labsolar III AG, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd), with cooling water to maintain the reaction solution at room temperature. Typically, 50mg of the prepared CNNS/CQDs or CNNS was dispersed into 100mL aqueous solution containing 10 vol% triethanolamine scavengers. 3wt% Pt co-catalyst was added by dissolving H2PtCl6 into the above mixed solution. Then, the suspension was degassed for 30min to remove air by employing a vacuum pump. The light source was a 300W Xe arc lamp (PLS-SXE 300/300UV, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co. Ltd., China) equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ>420nm). The visible light intensity was 100mWcm−2. The generated H2 was analyzed using an online gas chromatograph (GC7806, Beijing Shiweipuxin Analytical Instruments Co. Ltd, China) using a TCD detector and a 5A molecular sieve as the column and N2 as a carrier.

Photoelectrochemical characterization was conducted using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Chenhua Instruments, China) in a quartz cell with a standard three-electrode system. A platinum plate and a saturated Ag/AgCl were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For the preparation of working electrodes, the as-prepared CNNS and CNNS/CQDs composite were thoroughly dispersed in ethanol, and then cast onto FTO substrate. The film was dried at 80°C for 2h to obtain the CNNS and CNNS/CQDs thin film electrodes with an active area of 0.50×0.50cm2. 1.0M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as an electrolyte. The light source was a 500W Xe lamp (CHF-XM, Beijing Changtuo Technology Co. Ltd., China) equipped with an ultraviolet cutoff filter (λ>420nm) and the integrated visible light intensity was tuned to 100mWcm−2.

The morphology of the prepared CQDs was observed by TEM, which is shown in Fig. 1 A. It is observed that the CQDs with diameter of 2–10nm are uniformly distributed without visible aggregation. SEM image displays lamellar structure of CNNS, as illustrated in Fig. 1B, which was derived from the released gases (NH3 and HCl) from the decomposition of NH4Cl in the thermal polymerization process. As displayed in Fig. 1C, Pt nanoparticles were observed on the surface of CNNS after photocatalytic reaction. Upon coupling the CQDs with CNNS, the corresponding TEM and HRTEM images of CNNS/CQDs-7 are shown in Fig. 1D–G. A large number of CQDs are uniformly embedded on the surface of CNNS (Fig. 1D). The HRTEM images of CNNS/CQDs-7 reveal the different lattice spacings of 0.32 (Fig. 1F) and 0.22nm (Fig. 1G), corresponding to (002) crystallographic plane of graphitic carbon and (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene, respectively [39]. It is noted that the observed lattice spacings are the same as that of the pure CQDs. These results demonstrate that the CQDs have been successfully deposited onto the surface of CNNS to form CNNS/CQDs composites.

The phase structure of the prepared CNNS and CNNS/CQDs composites was characterized by XRD. Fig. 2 A displays the XRD patterns of the CNNS/CQDs hybrid photocatalysts with different contents of CQDs, and the pure CNNS. All the photocatalysts exhibited two distinct diffraction peak at 27.7° and 13.2°, which can be indexed to graphitic carbon nitride as the (002) and (100) crystal facets (JCPDS 87-1526). The strongest diffraction peak (002) at 27.7° corresponding to distance of 0.321nm reflects the inter-planar stacking of aromatic system, while the 13.2° peak is derived from the (100) in-plane repeated tri-s-triazine units [40]. It is noted that no diffraction peaks of CQDs can be observed in the CNNS/CQDs composites owing to low contents.

Fig. 2B displays the FTIR spectra of the pure CNNS, CQDs and CNNS/CQDs-7 composite. It can be clearly observed that the sample of neat CQDs shows obvious peaks at 2940, 2870 and 1720cm−1, corresponding to the skeletal vibration of sp2 and sp3 CH and −COOH, respectively [41,42]. The pure CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 displayed similar spectra, showing the introduction of CQDs does not change the chemical structure of the g-C3N4. In detail, the absorption peaks between 3600 and 3000cm−1 are attributed to NH stretching, few hydrogenated N atoms shown in the g-C3N4 nanosheets. The typical absorption peaks between 1700 and 1200cm−1 belong to CN and CN stretching vibration modes. The sharp breathing peak of triazine units at 810cm−1 also appears in both the CNNS and the CNNS/CQDs-7. The chemical structure of the as-prepared CNNS and CNNS/CQDs are basically in accordance with the g-C3N4.

To further illustrate the interaction between CNNS and CQDs, XPS was performed to analyze the surface chemical composition of CNNS/CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3 A shows that CNNS/CQDs-7 is mainly composed of carbon and nitrogen. The O 1s peak at 532.3eV is attributed to the absorbed water (Fig. 3B). Fig. 3C shows the N 1s spectrum, which displays four deconvoluted peaks at 404.5, 401.2, 399.8 and 398.8eV. The dominant N 1s signal peak at 398.8eV is attributed to sp2 hybridized aromatic N in the triazine units. The peak at binding energy of 399.8eV corresponds to the tertiary N in N(C) 3 or HN(C) 2. The weak peak located at 401.2eV is attributed to quaternary N bonded to three carbon atoms in the aromatic cycles [43,44]. As shown in the high resolution C 1s XPS spectra in Fig. 3D, the two peaks at 288.3 and 284.9eV are assigned to the sp2 carbon (NCN) and graphitic carbon (CC) [14,45]. The ratio of CC bonds and NCN changed from 0.217 to 0.262 after coupling with CQDs, indicating that the CQDs were adhered onto the surface of CNNS.

In order to analyze the optical properties of CQDs, UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of the CQDs were recorded. Fig. 4 A indicates that CQDs show strong optical absorption in the UV region, with a tail extending to the visible range. A typical absorption peak at 230nm, which is assigned to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains was also observed. Moreover, a weak absorption (shoulder) peak at around 320nm belongs to the n–π* transition of CO band, which is in agreement with previous reports [46,47]. In Fig. 4B, the CQDs exhibit strong emission peak under excitation by the light with wavelength from 300 to 380nm. The PL peak shows a slight red-shift along with the gradual intensity decreasing as the excitation wavelength alters from 400 to 500nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of pure CNNS and CNNS/CQDs hybrid photocatalysts with different contents of CQDs were shown in Fig. 5 A. Compare to pure CNNS, the light absorption in the UV–vis light region is greatly enhanced upon the increasing content of CQDs. The remarkable up-conversion properties are shown in the inset of Fig. 5. It can be seen that the emission band in the 500–550nm region under 600–800nm excitation makes the CQDs excellent light harvesters to enhance the light absorption of the CNNS. For the pure CNNS, the absorption edge occurs at about 440nm. After coupling with CQDs, the absorption edge of CNNS/CQDs exhibits red-shift in comparison to the pure CNNS. The bandgap of a semiconductor can be calculated with Tauc plot. Fig. 5B shows the curve of converted (αhν)γ versus hν from the UV–vis spectrum, in which α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively, and γ=1/2 for an indirect band-gap semiconductor. From the curve, the band-gap of CNNS decreases from 2.83 to 2.80eV after introducing CQDs, which implying that there may be subband energy levels between CNNS and CQDs in the bandgap of CNNS/CQDs composite. [48] This assumption is further proved by the PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 5C, one can see that both CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 display a wide emission peak at about 500nm under excitation at wavelength of 315nm, which corresponds to the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. Particularly, the PL emission intensity of CNNS/CQDs-7 decreases obviously, which suggests that the charge recombination is retarded by loading of CQDs.

The photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples was evaluated by photocatalytic production of H2 in a water-triethanolamine solution under visible light irradiation (λ>420nm) at room temperature. As indicated in Fig. 6 A and B, the H2 production was enhanced upon increasing irradiation time. The total H2 production was 151.0μmol over the pure CNNS in 4h, while it increased to 464.5μmol with 7mL CQDs loading onto CNNS, and reached the maximum there. The H2 evolution rate over CNNS/CQDs-7 was 116.1μmolh−1, which is three times of that over the pure CNNS (37.8μmolh−1). Further increasing the content of CQDs in the CNNS/CQDs composites resulted in lower production rate of H2. In such situation, excess CQDs not only weakened the light-absorption of CNNS, but also could be the recombination sites of electron-hole pairs. With comparison to the reported catalysts based on g-C3N4 and CQDs for photocatalytic generation of hydrogen, as shown in Table 1 , one can see that the CNNS/CQDs prepared in this study exhibits the highest production rate of H2. It is noted that without any scavenger, H2 was not generated over the CNNS/CQDs catalysts. In a previous study, Kang et al. reported the overall water splitting over the CDots/g-C3N4 catalysts synthesized by thermal condensation of urea mixed with CDots. On the catalysts, the g-C3N4 was reported to photocatalyze water splitting to H2 and H2O2. The H2O2 was further decomposed to O2 by CDots. In such process, the CDots must be distributed in the closet proximity to the active surface of the g-C3N4 grains to make the overall water splitting successively and synergistically [37]. In our work, the CNNS/CQDs composite was prepared by a hydrothermal reaction of CNNS and CQDs. The combination of CQDs with CNNS is not as strong as that reported. Thus, the H2O2 generated on the CNNS cannot be decomposed by CQDs immediately. The H2O2 generated by CNNS decreased with time due to poisoning of CNNS by H2O2. In the subsequent work, efforts are devoted to disclose various preparation approaches on their performance of the CNNS/CQDs catalysts for overall water splitting.

In addition, the stability and durability of the photocatalysts are also indispensable to their applications. To evaluate the long-term stability of the CNNS/CQDs hybrid photocatalyst, we performed a time-circle H2 generation experiment. Fig. 6C presents the H2 evolution as the time prolonged to 12h. As shown in Fig. 6C, no noticeable drop of H2 production was observed. The result indicated the high stability and durability of the CNNS/CQDs photocatalyst.

In order to study the photoinduced charge-transfer behavior, the photoelectrochemical response of the samples was examined. As shown in Fig. 7 A, liner sweep voltammagrams of the CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 electrodes in dark and under illumination were conducted. The CNNS/CQDs-7 electrode displayed enhanced photocurrent compared to the pure CNNS under illumination. In addition, the onset potential of the CNNS electrode was 0.25V, while the CNNS/CQDs-7 was about −0.2V, which is negatively shifted. It means that the photoelectrochemical response is enhanced because of more efficient transportation of the carriers.

The transient photocurrent responses of CNNS and CNNS/CQDs-7 during the repeated on/off cycles under UV–vis irradiation (100mWcm−2) at 0.8V vs Ag/AgCl were measured. As shown in Fig. 7B, the photocurrent density of pure CNNS was as low as 0.2μAcm−2. The CNNS/CQDs-7 sample shows two times as high as photocurrent density than that of the CNNS. The result is consistent with photocatalytic production of H2. The improved photocurrent density of CNNS/CQDs-7 can be attributed to the fact that the CQDs act as electron reservoirs to trap electrons emitted from CNNS and thus enhances the separation sufficiency of electron-hole pairs. The result is also in line with the PL analysis.

From the above results, the proposed enhanced photocatalytic H2-production and charge transfer mechanism over the CNNS/CQDs composite photocatalyst are schematically illustrated in Fig. 8 . Under visible light irradiation, the generated electron-hole pairs of the pure CNNS are prone to recombination. After modification by the CQDs, it can be found that the CNNS/CQDs composites showed higher photocatalytic performance than pure CNNS. The possible reasons are as follows. Firstly, when the CNNS is excited under visible light irradiation, the generated electrons from the conduction band of CNNS transferred to CQDs, which facilitates the efficient separation of electrons and holes, thus enhances the photocatalytic performance. Secondly, CQDs with low level in the composite act as a photosensitizer, making the CNNS absorb more visible light to denote the electrons to the conduction band of CNNS. As a result, the photocatalytic production of H2 is enhanced.

In summary, novel CNNS/CQDs hybrid photocatalysts were prepared by assembling CQDs with g-C3N4 nanosheets by facile sonochemical approach. The structure and photocatalytic production of H2 were studied. With the introduction of CQDs, the photocatalytic generation of H2 over CNNS/CQDs composites under visible light increased dramatically. The optimized composite catalyst, CNNS/CQDs-7 exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, with corresponding to H2 production rate of 116.1μmolh−1, which is three times of that over the CNNS. The dramatically improved photocatalytic performance was attributed to the fact that the CQDs act as electron reservoirs to trap the electrons for improving charge separation efficiency and light harvesters to enhance the visible light absorption. This study demonstrates that CQDs are active components for improving photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 and provides novel inspiration in developing g-C3N4-based hybrid photocatalysts by loading other quantum dots materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21273080) and Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (S2012010008383).
